The Oracle-bone inscriptions are considered to offer a significant insight into the religious thoughts of the Western Zhou dynasty. These inscriptions are the earliest known examples of Chinese writing, and they offer valuable information on the divinatory practices of the Zhou dynasty. The religion of the Zhou dynasty was characteristically polytheistic, and the people believed in an extensive pantheon of gods and goddesses. The Zhou people believed that the natural world was controlled by a group of deities, and they utilized oracle-bones to communicate with these gods. 

The Oracle-bones were bones or shells that were inscribed with divinatory messages. The bones were heated to create cracks, and these cracks were then interpreted as prophecies. The messages inscribed on the oracle-bones were typically requests for advice or predictions relating to issues such as farming, health, and war. The responses, written in a range of characters, mainly provided favorable or unfavorable answers.

Among the various deities that were worshipped during the Zhou dynasty, only a few are mentioned in the oracle-bone inscriptions. These deities include Shang Di, the god of heaven, and Di Jun, the god of earth. However, other gods and goddesses were also worshipped, including the deities associated with natural features such as rivers, mountains, and rocks. 

Another central aspect of the religious thoughts of the Western Zhou dynasty was the veneration of ancestors. The oracle-bone inscriptions reveal that the Zhou placed great importance on ancestor worship. Ancestors were seen as intermediaries between the people and the gods, and the people believed that they could gain favor from their ancestors by performing ceremonies and rituals. The Zhou believed that maintaining harmonious relationships with their ancestors was the key to achieving a good afterlife.

The Oracle-bone inscriptions have had a lasting impact on Chinese culture and history. They provide valuable information about the early development of the Chinese writing system and the divinatory practices of the Zhou dynasty. They have also given us an insight into the religious beliefs and practices of the Zhou people. Through these inscriptions, we can see the extent to which religion was a central feature of life in ancient China. The inscriptions help us to better understand the religious heritage of China and its impact on the country's culture and society.

Additionally, the Oracle-bone inscriptions have had a significant impact on the development of Chinese literature. The inscriptions inspired Chinese writers and poets throughout the ages, and they have been used as source material for many works of fiction and non-fiction. The inscriptions have also influenced the development of Chinese calligraphy. The ancient characters used in the inscriptions inspired the creation of new forms of writing, which eventually led to the development of the distinctive Chinese calligraphic style that we see today.

In conclusion, the Oracle-bone inscriptions of the Western Zhou dynasty offer a valuable insight into the religious thoughts and practices of ancient China. The inscriptions reveal the intricate polytheistic beliefs of the Zhou people, as well as their reverence for ancestors and the natural world. The Oracle-bone inscriptions have had a profound impact on Chinese history and culture, influencing the development of literature and calligraphy. They stand as a testament to the rich cultural heritage of China and continue to inspire and inform us today.

The Oracle-bone inscriptions, discovered in the early 20th century, date back to the Western Zhou dynasty (1046 BC–771 BC) in China. The inscriptions were made on turtle shells and the shoulder blades of domesticated animals, such as oxen, sheep, and pigs. The bones were used as a form of divination, in which a crack was created on the bone's surface by heating it with coal and interpreting the pattern as a message from the gods.

The Oracle-bone inscriptions were initially used by the Shang dynasty, and they continued to be used by the succeeding Western Zhou dynasty. The inscriptions provide valuable insights into the political and religious beliefs of the Zhou dynasty. The bones were commissioned by the kings and queens of the dynasty, as well as other nobles, and they were primarily concerned with matters such as divination, the selection of auspicious dates for ceremonies, and the appointment of officials.

The Oracle-bone inscriptions reveal the religious beliefs of the Zhou dynasty, which were deeply rooted in divination and ancestor worship. Some of the deities worshipped during the Zhou dynasty include Shang Di (the god of heaven), Di Jun (the god of earth), and T'ien (the god of the universe). The Zhou people believed that these gods controlled the natural world and that they could intervene in human affairs through divination.

In addition to the gods, the Zhou also worshipped their ancestors. The oracle-bones contain many references to ancestors, and they were used to communicate with the spirits of the deceased. Ancestors were believed to have supernatural powers and were considered to be intermediaries between the living and the gods. The Zhou believed that by performing ceremonies and offering sacrifices to their ancestors, they could gain their favor and ensure good fortune for themselves and their descendants.

The Oracle-bone inscriptions also shed light on the social and political structure of the Zhou dynasty. The inscriptions were commissioned by the ruling elite, including the kings and queens, as well as other members of the nobility. The bones provide us with a glimpse into the daily lives of these aristocrats, as well as the people who served them. The inscriptions include the names of various officials and craftsmen, as well as details about their duties and responsibilities.

In conclusion, the Oracle-bone inscriptions of the Western Zhou dynasty offer a fascinating insight into the religious, social, and political beliefs of ancient China. The inscriptions are important historical documents that have had a significant impact on Chinese literature, art, and culture. They have inspired many works of fiction and non-fiction, and they continue to inform and inspire researchers and scholars around the world. The Oracle-bone inscriptions remain a testament to the rich cultural heritage of China and serve as evidence of its long and fascinating history.

The oracle-bones were used to communicate with these gods, through which the Zhou received guidance and advice on different aspects of life, such as warfare, farming, and health.

Scholars have studied the Oracle-bone inscriptions of the Western Zhou dynasty extensively, recognizing their significance in the understanding of ancient Chinese culture and history. They are an essential source for the study of divination in ancient China, and they provide valuable insights into the political and religious lives of the ancient Zhou people.

The inscriptions are also significant in the development of Chinese writing and calligraphy. Scholars have traced the evolution of these inscriptions from the earliest forms of Chinese writing to the modern characters used today. They also continue to inspire contemporary Chinese calligraphers who study their calligraphic style to create new works, ushering in new dimensions to the practice.

Furthermore, historians and scholars have evaluated multiple theories associated with the origin and function of the divination inscriptions. Evidence suggests that divination offered the Zhou dynasty's ruling elite a language of communication with their gods, ancestors, and natural world, which facilitated critical aspects of governance, including military strategy and protection, decisions on appointments, and agricultural planning.

In teaching and learning, the inscriptions continue to fascinate scholars and students alike. Institutions of higher learning have increasingly included the Oracle-bone inscriptions in undergraduate and graduate programs as part of the curriculum, where students can learn about the sophisticated divination and calligraphy techniques and appreciate Chinese history and culture.

In conclusion, the Oracle-bone inscriptions of the Western Zhou dynasty have become an essential tool in the research, analysis, study, and teaching of ancient Chinese culture and history, including literature, art, religion, sociology, and politics. Their preservation, research, and interpretation are crucial to the appreciation of this magnificent cultural heritage, and scholars continue to make significant strides in these areas.
